title: Wet felted bag with handles
subtitle: A functional handbag-sized vessel felted around a resist with an integrated handle opening
excerpt: Wet felt a structured bag roughly 25 x 20 cm from 200 g of Corriedale roving using a large resist cut from bubble wrap. The handles are formed by opening slits in the wet felt before fulling is complete. The finished bag is firm enough to hold its shape when put down.
sourceNotes: Resist-felted bags are a standard intermediate project in textile guild and workshop instruction. The bubble-wrap resist approach is widely used in contemporary wet-felting practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A piece of bubble wrap cut to the desired bag shape and used as the internal resist during wet felting. The roving is wrapped around it on both sides, felted until the fibres hold, then the resist is removed through an opening cut or slit into the felt before fulling is complete.

content.text: The bag is formed around a

cut to 30 x 25 cm (the finished bag will be smaller after shrinkage). Corriedale is preferred over fine merino for bags because its coarser staple produces a firmer, more structured felt that holds its shape when the bag is put down.

items.name: Bubble wrap cut to 30 x 25 cm

content.text: Step 1: Layer roving on one side of the resist

Lay the bamboo mat flat. Place the bubble wrap resist on the mat (bubbles facing down). Cover the resist completely with overlapping strips of Corriedale in two perpendicular layers, extending about 5 cm beyond all edges. This overhang will wrap around to form the bag's other side.
Step 2: Fold overhang and layer the second side

Wet out the first side completely through the felting net, pressing firmly until saturated. Flip the resist over, fold the overhanging roving neatly around to the back of the resist, and add two more perpendicular roving layers on this back face. Wet out through the net.

Rub the surface in circular strokes through the net for 10 minutes per side, flipping the whole assembly and working both faces equally. The roving should begin to firm and the surface fibres lock together. Check at the edges: the overhang should be integrating into a continuous surface, not separating at the fold.
Step 4: Cut handle slits and remove the resist

Once the felt holds together without the net (it passes the pinch test), cut two parallel horizontal slits about 12 cm long near the top of the bag, one on each side, positioned symmetrically. Reach into the slit on each side and pull out the bubble wrap resist. The bag is now open at the handle slits and the top is an opening. Neaten the cut edges by rolling them inward slightly and rubbing to felt them closed.

Continue rolling on the bamboo mat, turning the bag 90 degrees between sessions, until it has shrunk to the desired dimensions and the felt is firm throughout. Rinse in cool water, press out moisture in a towel, and shape by stuffing with a plastic bag while damp. Allow to dry with the stuffing in place.

items.problem: The bag has a weak seam along the bottom fold where the two sides meet
items.cause: The overhang was not folded and pressed firmly enough before the second-side roving went on
items.fix: After folding the overhang, rub the folded edge firmly with a soapy palm before adding the second-side layers.

items.problem: The handle slits tear rather than cutting cleanly
items.cause: Scissors not sharp enough or felt not firm enough at the time of cutting
items.fix: Wait until the felt clearly passes the pinch test before cutting. Use sharp fabric scissors with long blades.

items.problem: The bag loses its shape when dried without stuffing
items.cause: Felt not fulled to a firm enough density before drying
items.fix: Continue rolling after the resist is removed until the bag walls feel firm and spring back when pressed. Stuff fully while damp.
